meta.title: OAI-3: Context Quality, Responsible Use, and Tool-Backed Workflows

questions.stem:
A support lead wants consistently useful answers from ChatGPT for refund-policy questions. Early outputs are vague because the requests omit the customer type, policy constraints, and expected response format.

Which change MOST directly improves output quality?

options.text: Add specific context, constraints, and the desired format to the prompt
options.explanation: Clear instructions and relevant context are the foundation of effective prompting.

options.text: Increase randomness so the model improvises more
options.explanation: Higher randomness does not compensate for missing instructions.

options.text: Replace all prompts with one-word requests
options.explanation: Under-specified prompts usually worsen relevance.

options.text: Rely on a later follow-up to clarify every missing detail
options.explanation: Repeated cleanup is less reliable than providing the needed context upfront.

questions.stem:
A legal operations team needs the model to return extracted contract obligations in a fixed JSON shape every time.

Which technique BEST supports that requirement?

options.text: Use Structured Outputs with a schema that defines the expected JSON fields
options.explanation: Structured Outputs are designed to make model responses conform to a defined schema.

options.text: Ask the model to be creative so it explores more formats
options.explanation: Creativity increases variability instead of enforcing a fixed shape.

options.text: Remove examples so the model has fewer constraints
options.explanation: Removing constraints reduces consistency.

options.text: Split the answer across multiple unrelated conversations
options.explanation: Separating context does not enforce schema compliance.

questions.stem:
An analyst receives a polished answer that includes a factual claim she cannot verify from her source materials.

What is the BEST next step?

options.text: Ask the model to cite or point to supporting evidence and verify the claim before using it
options.explanation: Outputs should be checked for factual support when accuracy matters.

options.text: Publish the answer because fluent wording implies accuracy
options.explanation: Fluency is not evidence.

options.text: Assume the model only states facts it has recently browsed
options.explanation: The model does not inherently validate every claim against live sources.

options.text: Lower the temperature after publishing the answer
options.explanation: Changing randomness later does not verify the current claim.

questions.stem:
A project manager likes the first draft overall, but it is too long and buries the action items.

Which follow-up is MOST effective?

options.text: Continue in the same conversation and ask for a shorter version that highlights action items
options.explanation: Iterative refinement is a standard way to improve length, focus, and formatting.

options.text: Start over with a different vendor before refining the draft
options.explanation: Switching vendors does not address a prompt-refinement issue.

options.text: Accept the first output because iteration rarely helps
options.explanation: AI drafts are meant to be iterated on.

options.text: Delete the earlier context so the model cannot refer to the draft
options.explanation: Discarding useful context can make revision harder.

questions.stem:
A hospital administrator wants to paste patient identifiers into a general-purpose chatbot to speed up drafting.

What is the MOST responsible guidance?

options.text: Avoid sharing unnecessary sensitive personal data and follow the organization’s approved privacy controls
options.explanation: Privacy-sensitive information should be minimized and handled under approved controls.

options.text: Paste the raw identifiers because faster drafting outweighs privacy concerns
options.explanation: Speed does not override privacy obligations.

options.text: Share the full record as long as the prompt is short
options.explanation: Short prompts can still expose sensitive data.

options.text: Use sensitive data freely whenever the output is internal only
options.explanation: Internal use alone does not remove data-handling risk.

questions.stem:
A finance team wants AI to approve customer credit decisions without any human review.

Which approach BEST aligns with responsible AI practice?

options.text: Keep a qualified human in the loop for consequential decisions and review outputs before action
options.explanation: High-stakes decisions need human oversight and accountability.

options.text: Let the model make final decisions whenever its wording sounds confident
options.explanation: Confidence in wording is not a control.

options.text: Skip review if the model has produced good answers in the past week
options.explanation: Past performance does not remove the need for review in consequential cases.

options.text: Use AI only if the decision can never be audited later
options.explanation: Lack of auditability makes the process riskier, not safer.

questions.stem:
A chief of staff needs meeting notes transformed into a concise status update with decisions, risks, and next actions.

Which prompt change BEST improves the result?

options.text: Specify the required sections and audience for the summary
options.explanation: Summary quality improves when the desired structure and audience are explicit.

options.text: Ask for a summary with no instructions about structure or audience
options.explanation: Missing structure leaves the model to guess.

options.text: Increase randomness so the notes become more diverse
options.explanation: Higher randomness is not a substitute for clear requirements.

options.text: Tell the model to respond however it prefers
options.explanation: Removing guidance reduces consistency.

questions.stem:
A consultant uses AI to break a complex migration into milestones, owners, and dependencies.

Which output format is MOST useful for execution?

options.text: A structured plan with ordered steps, owners, dependencies, and risks
options.explanation: Execution planning is stronger when tasks, sequencing, and ownership are explicit.

options.text: A poetic paragraph that mentions the migration at a high level
options.explanation: High-level prose is weaker for operational follow-through.

options.text: A response that avoids committing to any sequence
options.explanation: Avoiding sequence makes the plan hard to execute.

options.text: An answer that mixes planning details with unrelated brainstorming
options.explanation: Unfocused brainstorming reduces actionability.

questions.stem:
A sales application must look up current pricing from a trusted system before sending a quote.

Which design is BEST?

options.text: Give the model access to a tool or function that retrieves the latest approved pricing
options.explanation: Tool or function calls are the right way to fetch external, up-to-date data.

options.text: Ask the model to invent likely prices from memory
options.explanation: The model should not fabricate current business data.

options.text: Disable tool use and hope the model remembers current figures
options.explanation: Without retrieval the answer may be stale or wrong.

options.text: Use a static example price for all customers
options.explanation: Static placeholders are unsuitable for real quotes.

questions.stem:
A workflow lets the model call a ticketing tool that can create incidents in production systems.

Which control is MOST appropriate?

options.text: Validate the tool inputs and apply review or guardrails before high-impact actions
options.explanation: High-impact tool use should be bounded with schemas, validation, and review controls.

options.text: Allow any tool call because models never make unsafe requests
options.explanation: Models can still produce incorrect or unsafe tool arguments.

options.text: Hide the tool description so the model cannot reason about it
options.explanation: Hiding the contract weakens reliable tool selection.

options.text: Combine all possible tools into one vague action with no schema
options.explanation: Vague tools increase ambiguity and misuse risk.
